author: @DavidPaulNewtonScott
text: I have come to the conclusion that for me the best thing is to have a horizontal turbine along the roof of my house in Portugal. However with a bit of a diffusing  nossel on it. This raises the possibility of placing solar modules on the diffuser all that wind will cool the pv modules. An interesting point about pv modules my favourite company is sunstore. I will be using the 5W 12V modules. This seems nuts, but if you break one (very unlikely), you just lose 5W. They will be stiffer and will cool better. I may mount them invisibly in trees.

author: @401ksolar
text: I recall making the point of closing the gap on Lukes oil drum windmill design,  this was suggested because of my decades of tinkering with both bird choppers vs bird repellent windmills, however just closing the gap was only a start, ended up using a heavy 6" pipe for the center of the rotor creating a compression point inside the scoop which greatly improved total torque to the generator/air compressor, still wonder if Luke ever improved his structure for that heavy oil drum rotor experiment?

author: @Berend-ov8of
text:
Hello Mr. Murray-Smith,
With some luck, you're the guy I'm looking for.
In my research I stumbled across something that refuses to let go of me.
It's the idea that air can not slow down.
According to this notion, any molecule or atom in air (or any gas for that matter) can only travel at the speed of sound in relation to its point of origin.
So much so in fact, that it is that velocity that defines a gas.
There appear to be all sorts of scientific arguments against it, but they all seem a bit far fetched and theoretical, whereas many observed phenomena and simple logic support it.
Air can not travel faster since the speed of sound is an escape velocity and thus not related to the total magnitude of the force that created it.
It can't go slower either because then it simply doesn't escape.
The thing we call airflow is merely the dominant direction of the resulting movement.
It explains why increase of airflow always coincides with decrease of pressure for example.
It opens a whole world of new interesting possibilities.
Theoretically it allows for a turbine to be powered by the thermal energy of the air passing through it, just to name one.
Since you seem to cherish free thinking, I was wondering what you think of it.

text: Great video Robert, food for thought. I was thinking what would different types of spiral effect be like on a car Turbocharger? They are very balanced and are quite easily aquired. You can blow on a turbo intake and it moves very easily and it's also capable of having an exceedingly high RPM.   I'm also looking at posibilities of using something like a roots blower to compress air and recycle it back into a turbine. (but sadly only thinking, not much time to try it out.) Still repairing my compressor.

author: @DirkLarien
text:
Hello sir, 
could you please explore the idea of composing rotor magnets of various shapes in order to maximize magnetic flux on one side. I noticed recently when i bough few magnets of various sizes when they were stacked in the box, theses two stacks barely attracted one another an when i was playing with those two stacks at certain angles and configurations - like splitting them into several stacks, sliding or rotating and so on..... they attracted each other in wildly varying intensity in some configurations.
So i went and searched around. And i think in a very crude way i stumbled into a this concept of Halbach array.
